methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $4M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 11 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Grain Weevil is a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Ben Johnson that has raised $3.5M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a broader comp universe of nine private robotics firms and one public comparable, though a narrow peer set of same-stage, adjacent-stage robotics companies with sufficient data density was unavailable for direct median benchmarking.

The Stage-Progression method is applicable and forms the sole quantitative anchor for this analysis. Using total capital raised ($3.5M) as a proxy for current valuation and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical of Seed-to-next-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $9M. This method assumes Grain Weevil's capital efficiency and risk profile align with sector norms at this stage.

The Peer-Median approach is not applicable; while 11 robotics companies exist in the broader comp set, none met the narrow filters of identical or adjacent stage with similar valuation tier—a minimum of three datapoints was required. This limitation prevents triangulation via multiple methods and increases reliance on the stage-based model. The analysis supports a valuation range of $7M (low), $9M (median), and $10M (high), with the median as the most defensible point estimate. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-set peer benchmarking and the sensitivity of results to stage-transition multiplier assumptions.
